speaker: interviewer
text: Getting up to speed. OK, I'm recording slate and go ahead Linda.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's December 11th, 1995 and today we're going to be interviewing Mendel, Mani, Flaster at his home in San Diego, California. And I'm Linda Roth. The interview will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrific. Can you tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mendel Flaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spells M-E-N-D-E-L F-L-A-S-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born April the 23rd, 1920 in Grybow. That's a little town not far from Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Ezak Wolf Flaster. You want me to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's E-Z-A-K W-O-L-F-- Flaster-- F-L-A-S-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baila. B-A-I-L-A. Baila Flaster. Her maiden name was Birkenfeld. It's B-I-R-K-E-N-F-E-L-D-- Birkenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had three more brothers and two sisters. The oldest one was Sara-- was spells S-A-R-A. Later was one of my brothers, Naten. What is N-A-T-E-N. Chaim-- is C-H-A-I-M. And later was a girl-- Rebeka. It's R-E-B-E-K-A. And mine other brother was Mosez-- M-O-S-E-Z. Mosez.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in this lineup of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest-- I was the last of the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the town that you grew up in? What was it's name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grybow. It spells G-R-Y-B-O-W. Grybow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, when I was in Poland, it was a little town. It's-- it was a lot of the Jewish people. It was several synagogues over there. And it was a very nice little town. It was very clean, beautiful. We had a beautiful, beautiful lake running through middle of the town. What the lake was coming from the mountains. And it was very, very nice. And it was the-- in this city, the majority was Jewish people. But around the city, of course, we were the minority. But it was a nice little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house-- we had a old house what was from generations handed down to my mother from my grandparents. But that old house was so old already that we had to demolish it, and build another house. My-- one of my brothers was working as a very good place in Krynica-Zdroj. This is another city not far from us. And he made very good money. And he's helped to build a new house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved in in the house two years before the war-- was a brand new house, was very, very beautiful, very comfortable, and very modern. But when we lived only two years in it, the war broke out. And about a year later, the Germans throwed us out from the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was already in the camps. But my mother had to live in someplace else, because that was on the main street. And all the Jewish people had to moved out from the main streets in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what your house-- how many rooms it had and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our house had a living room and two-- two rooms, two bedrooms, with a nice kitchen, and another little room to put away things. It had a cellar, and it had also a top-- how you-- you call this? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Where you go up on steps-- how you call this? You know, what is on top on the house? You know, what we had over there to put away things. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: An attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: A attic-- it was a nice attic. Actually we could live in it. It was a nice attic there. It was a very nice, modern house. At that time, was very modern-- it was very, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you, excuse me, describe your schooling as you started as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started at the Jewish school-- I started by three years old. The Polish school by five years. When the-- I started the Polish school, that's right away that you could-- I could feel the anti-Semitism in the classes. Because it-- over there it was a separate school for boys, a separate school for girls. And the boys there were very nasty to the Jewish boys, because we were the minority. And they picked on us on the breaks, and when we were-- was going home from school, they always were picking on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to actually fight for your rights, you know? But they would leave you alone. We had different misagreement, different fights, and things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the worst thing was when we came to the third grade. The third grade was very hard to pass, because you started learning different things like the geography, [NON-ENGLISH], and all that items when was very hard to pass. The Polish boys-- they're not too smart on that, and they couldn't pass the third grade. And because they couldn't pass, they flunk about two, three years. And when we came in, we were little boys. You know, we were only about eight years or nine years, some of them. When they were already three, four years older from us, so they knew already the routine-- when they can pick on us, when they can beat us up, and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one time the teacher took us on the out someplace over there to visit some things. On the way back-- this was an outing-- on the way back he went home, because he lived not far from there, and let us students by ourselves. So they were prepared with knives and with stones to beat us up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my luck was, you know, that I was with another boy what was very, very strong. The whole family-- they were very, very strong. And no matter how little he was, but he was a very strong young man. And the fight starts on me and the other boy were fighting about 10 or 15 guys. Luckily enough, we didn't got hurt, only I got a cut with a knife right here, what I have still the mark here. And we beat up the other guys. Next day, the-- the police were there, and the mayor with the principal and all the teachers with the parents. And we told them what's happened. So over there in Poland, the principal, he had the right to really beat up on the boys. And if they didn't behave, they took him into his office, and he really punished him very seriously. And that was the time-- and that principal, he was a very righteous man, a very nice person. He's seen, you know, the thing what they did. He didn't like it. And he really punished them very seriously. Since then, we had to live with peace. We didn't have to fight for our rights.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you started a Jewish school at the age of three. What kind of things did you learn there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you learn the ABC. The first years, you learn the ABC. And you progressing where you're going, higher and higher. And-- it's-- the thing is within Europe, a boy you had to learn very, very hard. For instance, in the morning we-- we went to Polish school at 7:30 to 1 o'clock in the afternoon. 1 o'clock, we came home, I ate lunch, and I went back to the Jewish school till 8 o'clock in the evening. 8 O'clock in the evening when we come home, sometimes I could eat, or sometimes I was so tired that I couldn't eat. I went to bed, and I got up about 3 o'clock in the morning to make my homework to the Polish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a constant thing day in and day out, seven days a week. We didn't have any rest. The only rest, what it was actually was a Saturday. Also, we had to go to the synagogue in the morning. And in afternoon, we had to go to the Jewish school to learn for a few hours. So we didn't have too much rest. But we had to learn, and we did. And many-- you know-- I can now appreciate that I had to work so hard. I had to learn, because, you know, the knowledge, what I have, it is very important to me. I can share it now in different ways, my knowledge, what I learned when I was a young man. Which languages did you speak in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, we speak Polish. At home, we speak Jewish. In the Jewish school, we speak Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you see a young man like myself, I had-- we had to learn three languages right from the beginning. And that's rather nice because it is always good to know different languages. What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fun?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes we got out on a Sunday, out from the school, from the Jewish school for a couple hours earlier if we behaved and we knowed everything, if we learned good. So they gave us a couple hours of break. So we went to the park and we played soccer. So that was our fun. But we didn't have too much fun. We had to constantly to learn, to-- really we were pushing, pushing, pushing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to the Polish school seven years, but I finished the public school. Later, I went to the Jewish school. I finished when I was 16 years of age-- I finished high schooling. But I went to the yeshiva. What that is like a college, you know, and I finished that. I needed two more semesters to go to a bigger yeshiva, to a bigger school, that I could have the papers as a rabbi. Two more semesters. But I didn't go, because my father was poor. He couldn't pay for me, you know, in a big school like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I couldn't go over there, so I took apprenticeship as a tailor. I-- I went to another city to learn to be a tailor. And I was there three years, finished the tailoring, the apprenticeship, so the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, so the-- I already started making money, because the three years was finished. But the war broke out, and the-- right away the Germans said-- we went away, and later we come back. The Germans took us right away to work in the city. And that's what is my schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to when you were a young boy before the war. Can you tell me what your father did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a shoemaker. He had ready-made shoes, what he was making. He had a little stall and making ready-made shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us your relationship with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the relationship with the parents was very good because we were very-- with the discipline, we-- the father had the first and last word, you know, in the house. And it was very good relationship, very close. But the-- the thing is that I was so little at home. Because, as I told you before, I spent my whole young years learning. And later, when I went away to another city to be in apprenticeship, again, the only time when I came home when I was with my father and mother was only on the holidays, on Pesach, and on the high holidays. But there's very little that I could really have like he-- you know, a father goes out to the beach with their children, and plays ball with the children, and things like this. We didn't have this. Even though we were close-- we loved each other. But still, I was so occupied with different things, what-- I was very little at home. I slept at home for a few hours, and I went out to school. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special memory of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I have a very good memories of my father and mother-- more even my mother than my father. Because with my mother I was more. My father was more-- also occupied more. And my mother was at home. So my memories with my mother is very dear. And I still remember things that my mother used to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, my mother was a lady, a very pious lady. You know what pious is? Very orthodox. And she was very smart, very intelligent, and she could foresee things, what will happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: She told me things before the war that I remember today-- every word of it. And everything she said, it happens. I would think, like she would, somebody would tell her-- she knew everything. She was so smart. And she could foresee the things what that will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your home a religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Very, very religious. Very religious, because we kept our Shabbat when it was without a question. And my father went to the services every morning, in the morning, and in the evening-- every day, seven days a week. And it was kept very kosher, 100%. And, oh, it was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mom a good cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: The best. She was a very good cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your favorite thing she made for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, in Europe, life was very hard. And every food she cooked was your favorite food because you didn't got these snacks, you know, like you have here between meals. You came to the meal, you were hungry enough that you ate a s good appetite. And everything tasted perfectly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- well, sure there was favorite, like my mother used to make very good fish. She used to make very good-- you know, bake the gooses, and things like that, it was very, very nice. And the-- oh, there's all kind of food, you know. But I still like it today, even.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife makes it for me that's very close to my mother. Oh, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special memory of a particular holiday when you were growing up before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Particular holiday before the war-- well, you see, the nicest holiday was Pesach. Because for Pesach, the whole family came together because all our children were spread to different cities, different jobs, and different schools we were going to. So the-- on the Pesach, we all came together, and we spend together. And for Pesach, the different food what there is, it is very, very special-- very nice. Because, you know, you don't see this a whole year but the food you preparing for Pesach. And it's just something hard to explain how beautiful it was. We had such a good time to be together, even the many times where we argue-- discuss, not argue-- we're discussing things. Me and mine brothers, and my brother that is now in Paris, France, he'd always discuss things with my sister. They didn't see eye to eye, but it was in a very nice matter, not violent, by far. It's just a discussion that we had in the different ways. And it was very pleasant, very pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my bar mitzvah, when I got to be 13 years in Poland, you don't make parties like here. I got the-- to read to the Torah. I got-- I got a big pack of cigarettes, but I give cigarettes to everybody. You know, this was the whole thing, you know, on the bar mitzvah. They didn't make any parties like here. That-- that wasn't in style in Poland. You just said-- bought cigarettes, you give it to the people. Not on Shabbat, obviously, you know, just in the weekdays. You give the cigarettes out, and everybody wish you mazel tov. And that's That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you wore?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I wore?

speaker: interviewer
text: What you wore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That I can not remember. I cannot remember what I wear. That was too far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a teenager, did you belong to any clubs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did belong, when I was already a-- a teen, well-- by-- well, it was not teen. It was about 10 years old. But I joined a Zionist organization, Ha-No'ar ha-Zyyoni. And I, since I remembered the time, I always liked Israel because I learned in the Jewish school about Israel so much that I practically know Israel by heart. And I liked Israel very, very much. And then, so as soon I could join the Zionist organization, I joined it. Even mine teacher in the Jewish school didn't like it at all. I had to be discreet. You know, but I went to their meetings. If I had the time to go to certain times, you know. I had to play hooky a little bit, you know, to go over there on the certain meetings what we had. But I liked the Zionist organizations all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do at a typical meeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the meetings, we-- we were learning Hebrew, you know, the modern Hebrew, not the Hebrew from the Old Testament. Just the modern Hebrew we learned. We learned Hebrew songs-- we were dancing. You know the different, like, the Horah and things like this. And, of course, the history from Israel, you know, how it happened, what is in Israel-- from Keren Kayemet, Keren Hayesod, and all the things that we learned in the Zionist organization. And, of course, they pick people that they go on different outings, [NON-ENGLISH] they called this. Prepare them to be in a kibbutz, and to prepare them to be in Israel. They prepared us, you know, that the time will come that may-- we will go to Israel. It was rather interesting. It was very nice. I liked it, because it was something different. But, of course, something more to my schedule.

speaker: interviewer
text: As I didn't had enough, you know? But I never said no to nothing. I always liked to do more than average. When you were a teenager, did you have a girlfriend? Did you date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Matter of fact, I had one girl, but she had, I'm sure, I-- her father was a-- a lawyer. But it was, you know, I was a young man. We liked each other. When we come to dancing, she picked me always to dance, you know. But I danced with her. But I didn't have any-- you know, we didn't have the-- the time for it. And not the-- the mind to it, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, what were your dreams for yourself when you looked into the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you that we were so occupied with our life, with our learnings, with our going to school there, going to school there, that we didn't think of the future. The future was day by day. When we lived only day by day, we didn't know what the next day will bring. And we didn't care because, I know today I have to go to the-- this school, and to this school, and to do this, and to do this. If I finished the Polish school, then later I was in the yeshiva. We study at the, you know, the Talmud. We study at the 12, 15 hours a day. We started early morning, when it come time in the morning to say our prayers, we stop studying, and we say our prayers. And we didn't ate nothing, and just went back to the Talmud, you know, to the studying. Because you see, you understand, to study in the Talmud it's very much involved. It involves what you have to look at a part of that and part of the [NON-ENGLISH] and something else, in order to prepare the different ideas and come out, you know, to the [NON-ENGLISH], and to know what you're learning about, what you understand. It is not simple. It is very mind-boggling to learn the Talmud. It was very deep, deep thoughts-- deep things to answer. It is very, very hard. Of course, when you learn, and you understand, it is very rewarding. It is very rewarding. I can tell you one thing that I now appreciated much more to my father, my parents, what they let me go to the yeshiva and to learn. Because it was so rewarding for me. A matter of fact, I can tell you what-- what that helped me a lot in my darkest days in my life, that helped me to survive, helped me, you know, to be a human being. To be there to myself, to do the things that other people didn't think that can be done. And I did it. And I'm very appreciative even now what I learned. I-- I can-- you know, go to the-- any synagogue and-- and be the, you know, a hassan, be a cantor, and they can tell you things and to-- say their prayers. And I-- and I understand every word what I'm saying. That's the most important thing. When you say a prayer that you understand what you're saying. That can come from your heart, not only from the mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said at 16 you left to become a tailor apprentice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ma'am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us what that entailed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to start being a tailor, it's very, very hard, because my father had to pay the boss to take me in, just to occupy a chair for me. It had to be paid. On top of that, I didn't got paid for three years. Only, you know I worked. I started working where they teach me, you know, how to do this and how to do this. But later, when I was good, after a year time, when I worked already as a tailor-- so my boss liked me very much, that he start giving me lunches. Mind you, start giving me lunches, but I didn't have to come home to eat lunch.
